1: Salvē (Hello!) = "A friend is a second self"—Cicero
2: Rēs conclāvis scholāris (Classroom things) = "Ere the student congeal, tirelessly we must shape him on the potter's wheel." —Persius
3: Sententiae scholārēs (Classroom phrases) = “Neither let the youngest refuse to study philosophy, nor the oldest weary of it." —Epicurus
4: Ubi? (Where?) = "Where is the wise? Where is the scribe?" — 1 Corinthians 1:19-21
5: Unde oriundus? (Where are you from?) = "He who dwells everywhere, Maximus, dwells nowhere." —Martial
6: Quī sumus? (Who are we?) = "I am a philosopher." —Pythagoras
7: Nationēs (Countries) = “It is good to obey the laws of one’s country.” —Gyaecis
8: Audīre et loquī (Listen and speak) = "A man should listen!" —Plutarch
9: Miscellānea = “They have learned to speak from others, not from themselves.” —Cicero, Tusc. Quaes, v. 36.
10: Monstrā mihi (Show me) = “Show me good medicine, and I will take it.” —Horace, xvii. I.
11: Aperī/Claude (Open/Closed) = “Then at last truth issues from the heart; the visor’s gone, the man remains.” —Lucretius, iii.  57.
12: Mē vocant (My name is) = "Let the wise man bear the name of a madman if he seek wisdom more than is sufficient." —Horace
13: Edere (To eat) = “Democritus’ cattle eat his corn and spoil his fields, whilst his soaring mind ranges abroad without the body.” —Horace, Ep., i, 12, 12.
14: Dā mihi (Give me) = “O Varus! give me back my legions!” —Augustus Caesar
15: Quot? (How many?) = "How many condemnations have I seen more criminal than the crimes themselves?" —Cicero
16: Partēs corporis (Body parts) = "If his legs fail him, he fights on his knees." —Seneca, De Providentia, c. 2.
17: Tōtum diem discō (I work all day... a dialogue medley) = “My horse must work according to my step.” —Propertius, iv.
18: Tempestās (Weather) = “And to protect from the weather nearly all things are clothed with skin, or hair, or shells, or bark, or some such thing.” —Lucretius, iv. 936.
19: Umbram videō (I see a ghost) = “Let us pluck life’s sweets, ‘tis for them we live: by and by we shall be ashes, a ghost, a mere subject of talk.” —Persius, Sat., v. 151.
20: Quōmodo tē habēs (How do you feel?) = "He designed not to throw down Orodes as he fled, but to give him a wound unseen...” —Aeneid, x. 732.
21: Estne (Is it...?) = "Is it to be emperor? Is this your goal?" —Augustine
22: Imperātīva (Commands) = "Agesilaus sent his children to Sparta to learn how to obey and to command.” —Plutarch, Life of Agesilaus, c. 7.
23: ī/Venī (Come/Go) = “Fortune, I have got the better of thee, and have made all the avenues so sure thou canst not come at me.” —Cicero, Tusc. Quaes., v. 9.
24: Unde venīs? (From where do you come?) = “From where the sun rises to where it sets, there is no one whose acts can compare with mine” - Cicero, Tusc. Quaes., v. 17.
25: Exī/Intrā (Exit/Enter) = “A reasonable exit.” —Diogenes Laertius, Life of Zeno
26: Ad hortum (to the garden... ablatives) = When death comes, I want him to find me planting my cabbages, but not worrying about him or my unfinished garden. —Michel de Montaigne
27: Urbis Loca (Urban places) =  “Amidst that smother Neptune holds his place, / Below the walls’ foundation drives his mace, / And heaves the city from its solid base." —Virgil, Book 2, 5.1
28: Ī ad (I go to...) =  “Amidst that smother Neptune holds his place, / Below the walls’ foundation drives his mace, / And heaves the city from its solid base." —Virgil, Book 2, 5.1
29: Ad Ōstium (Going out) = “That I may go out better...” —Plato (when asked why he studies philosophy at the end of his life)
30: Review Dialogue Medley (Ōstium pulsā) = “When I review, I see one passage after another that should be erased.” —Ovid
31: Review Dialogue Medley (Cape, ede!) = “When I review, I see one passage after another that should be erased.” —Ovid
32: Review Dialogue Medley (Eō/Ī/It) = “When I review, I see one passage after another that should be erased.” —Ovid
33: Review Dialogue Medley (Stephanus et Magister) = “When I review, I see one passage after another that should be erased.” —Ovid
34: Review Dialogue Medley (Tiberia et pluvia) = “When I review, I see one passage after another that should be erased.” —Ovid
35: Prīmum, Deinde (First, Then) = "Tempora mutantur, et nōs mutāmur in illīs"—Ovid
36: Cape, Ede (Take, Eat) = "Nihil est in intellectū quod nōn prius fuerit in sensū"—Aristotle
38: Mūnera (Identity) = "Homo sum, humānī nihil ā mē alienum putō"—Terence
41: Dēmōnstrātīva Plūrālia (Hae, Hī, Haec) = Haec sunt quae sunt... “These things are what they are”
42: Familiā Neptis (The Granddaughter’s Family) = Prima societās in ipsā coniugatione est — “The first partnership is in marriage itself” — Cicero
43: Dē Cibīs (About Food) = De gustibus nōn est disputandum — “There is no disputing about tastes” — classical proverb
44: Placet / Nōn Placet (Likes & Dislikes) = Suum cuique placet — “Each person likes his own thing” — Cicero
45: Imperātīvus Plūrālis (Plural Imperative) = Imperāre sibi maximum imperium est — “To command oneself is the greatest command” — Seneca
46: Imperātīvus Plūrālis (Commands & Movement) = Festīnā lente — “Make haste slowly” — Augustus
47: Imperātīvus Plūrālis (Adverbs & Action) = Ars longa, vita brevis — “Art is long, life is short” — Hippocrates
48: Imperātīvus Plūrālis (Movement & Direction) = Omnēs viās Rōmam dūcunt — “All roads lead to Rome” — medieval proverb
49: Nōndum (Not Yet) = Dum spīrō, spērō — “While I breathe, I hope” — Cicero
50: Nōndum (Not Yet — Exīre & Ōstia) = Tempus fugit — “Time flies” — Virgil
51: Quid (Direct Object Practice) = Quid est veritās? — “What is truth?” — Pontius Pilate
52: Hunc / Hanc / Hoc (Direct Object Demonstratives) = Hic et nunc — “Here and now” — classical phrase
53: Hās / Haec / Hōs (Plural Direct Objects) = Haec olim meminisse iuvābit — “One day it will be pleasing to remember these things” — Virgil
54: Quam / Quem / Id (Personal Pronouns as D.O.) = Nosce tē ipsum — “Know thyself” — Socrates
55: Quī? Quid? Quōs? Quās? (Identification) = Quis custodiet ipsōs custōdēs? — “Who will guard the guards themselves?” — Juvenal
56: Hunc, Illam, Hoc, Illud (Demonstrative Objects) = Illud quod saepe audīmus, novum est — “That which we often hear is new” — Seneca
57: Illōs, Illās, Illum (Those — Direct Objects) = Illī sunt nōbīs — “Those men belong to us” — classical-style phrasing
58: Clāmāre et Loquī (Speaking & Calling Out) = Vox populī, vox Deī — “The voice of the people is the voice of God” — medieval proverb
59: Imperātīva Corporis (Body Commands) = Mens sana in corpore sānō — “A healthy mind in a healthy body” — Juvenal
60: Dare, Capere, Pōnere (Giving & Taking) = Dō ut dēs — “I give so that you may give” — Roman legal phrase
61: Quaerere Librōs (Seeking Books) = Quaerite et inveniētis — “Seek and you will find” — Biblical Latin
62: Quis? Quem? Cui? Quibus? (Cases & Roles) = Homo sum — “I am a human being” — Terence
63: Licet, Sinere (Permission) = Licet omnibus — “It is permitted to all” — classical-style
64: Cūr? (Cause) = Nihil sine causā — “Nothing without a cause” — Seneca
65: Conclāvia Domūs (Rooms of the House) = Domus sua cuique est tūta — “Each man’s home is safe for him” — Cicero
66: Actionēs Frequentes et Praeteritae (Frequent vs Past Actions) = Consuetūdō altera nātūra est — “Habit is a second nature” — Cicero
67: Dē Amōre et Studiō et Admirātiōne (Love, Interest, Admiration) = Amor vincit omnia — “Love conquers all” — Vergil
68: Dē “Quandō” et Adverbiīs Temporālibus (When/Time) = Tempus fugit — “Time flies” — Vergil
69: Tempora Diēī et Nōctis (Times of Day) = Omnia tempus habent — “All things have their time” — Ecclesiastes
70: Praeteritus Singularis I (Basic Singular Past Forms) = Facta manent — “Deeds remain” — Ovid
71: Praeteritus Singularis II (Movement, Following, Returning) = Iter facit sapientem — “Travel makes one wise” — proverb
72: Praeteritus Singularis III (Hands, Eating, Drinking, Up/Down) = Manus manum lavat — “One hand washes the other” — Seneca
73: Praeteritus Singularis IV (Doors, Windows, Before / Now / Then) = Initium dimidium factī — “The beginning is half the deed” — Horace
74: Praeteritus Plūrālis (Plural Past Forms) = Concordia rēs parvae crescunt — “Through harmony small things grow” — Sallust
75: Dē Multiplicibus Locīs (Complex Locations) = Omnia in locō suō — “Everything in its place” — proverb
76: Dē Multiplicibus Locīs (In Urbe) = Omnēs viae Rōmam dūcunt — “All roads lead to Rome” — proverb
77: Quōmodo dē Quālitāte Interrogēmus (Figūrae) = Ars imitātur nātūram — “Art imitates nature” — Aristotle
78: Quālis? (Qualitas Rerum) = Forma bonum fragile est — “Beauty is a fragile good” — Ovid
79: Quālis? (Personae et Qualitātēs) = Nosce tē ipsum — “Know thyself” — Delphic maxim
80: Cuius Colōris? (Colors) = Color omnibus est commune bonum — “Color is a common good to all” — Seneca
81: Quālitātēs (Qualities / Opposites) = Virtus in mediō stat — “Virtue stands in the middle” — Aristotle
82: Vestīmenta et Imperātīvī (Clothing and Commands) = Vestis virum reddit — “Clothing makes the man” — Erasmus
83: Numerī XI–XXX (Numbers 11–30) = Numerī rērum modum ostendunt — “Numbers show the measure of things” — Cicero
84: Emere, Vendere, Quantī? (Buying, Selling, Price) = Pretium rerum non ex verbīs sed ex usu pendet — “The price of things depends not on words but on use” — Seneca
85: Dē Officiīs et Opportunitāte (Obligation and Convenience) = Officium est quod faciendum est — “Duty is that which must be done” — Cicero
86: Tē Decet / Tē Nōn Decet (What is Fitting / Not Fitting) = Decet hominem honeste vivere — “It befits a man to live honorably” — Cicero
87: Monita et Vetita (Advice and Prohibitions) = Monēre est docēre — “To advise is to teach” — Seneca
88: Dē Prōnōminibus Reflexīs (Reflexive Pronouns) = Nōsce tē ipsum — “Know thyself” — Delphic maxim
89: Dē Possessiōne (Possession) = Omnia mea mecum portō — “I carry all my things with me” — Bias
90: Dē Possessiōne Singularī (Singular Possession) = Sua cuique — “To each his own” — Cicero
91: Dē Possessiōne Plūrālī (Plural Possession) = Res suās quisque amat — “Each person loves his own things” — Terence
92: Familia (Family) = Omnium societātum nulla praestantior est quam familia — “Of all associations none is more excellent than the family” — Cicero
93: Familia Latiōr (Extended Family) = Propinquī sunt quasi pars animae — “Relatives are like part of the soul” — Cicero
94: Salūtātiōnēs et Cubitum Ire (Greetings and Going to Bed) = Somnus est imāgō mortis — “Sleep is the image of death” — Cicero
95: Salūtātiōnēs ad Propinquōs (Extended Family) = Sanguis nōn mentitur — “Blood does not lie” — proverb
96: Cōnsobrīnī et Patruēlēs (Cousins) = Amīcī sunt familia electa — “Friends are the family we choose” — adapted
97: Dē Qualitāte et Statū (Appearance vs Reality) = Species fallit — “Appearance deceives” — proverb
98: Prūdēns et Stultus (Wise and Foolish) = Stultitia est contrāriā sapientiae — “Foolishness is the opposite of wisdom” — Cicero
99: Perterritus et Adflīctus (Terrified and Distressed) = Miserere miserōrum — “Have pity on the miserable” — proverb
100: Adflīctus Plūrālis (Plural Distress) = Miseria multōs afficit — “Misery affects many” — proverb
101: Motus et Actiōnēs (Movement and Actions) = Actiōnēs hominem ostendunt — “Actions reveal the man” — proverb
102: Cibus et Mēnsa (Food and Table) = Cibus est necessārius — “Food is necessary” — proverb
103: Dē Āctiōnibus Imminentibus (Ongoing Actions) = Dum agis discis — “While you act you learn” — proverb
104: Participia Plūrālia (Plural Participles) = Dum agimus discimus — “While we act we learn” — proverb
105: Lac et Ebrietās (Milk and Drunkenness) = Ebriētās est voluntāria insānia — “Drunkenness is voluntary madness” — Seneca
106: Futūrum Proximum (Near Future) = Tempus fugit — “Time flies” — Vergil
107: Dē Multiplicibus Mōtibus (Complex Movements) = Ars est celāre artem — “Art is to conceal art” — proverb
108: Mōtus Naturālis et Vīs (Natural Motion and Force) = Vīs movet — “Force moves” — adapted
109: Volō aliquid / aliquem aliquid facere = Velle est posse — “To want is to be able” — proverb
110: Dīcō… Quaerō utrum… an… = Verba volant — “Words fly” — proverb
111: Rogō aliquid; Cōnsentiō = Quaerēns invenit — “He who seeks finds” — proverb
112: Poscō, Cōnstituō, Imperō, Efficiō = Imperium est mandātum — “Command is an order” — adapted
113: Rogō quōmodo; Doceō; Discō = Docendō discimus — “By teaching we learn” — Seneca
114: Dīcō aliquid factūrum esse = Futura prōspice — “Look to the future” — proverb
115: Cōgitāre et Dubitāre (Thinking and Doubting) = Dubium sapientiae initium — “Doubt is the beginning of wisdom” — Descartes (adapted Latin)
116: Hērī Hodiē Crās (Time) = Tempora mutantur — “Times change” — Ovid
117: Vidētur (It Seems) = Vidētur quod est — “It seems what it is” — adapted
118: Dē Cupiditātibus et Vōtīs (Desire and Wishes) = Utinam quod vīs habeās — “If only you may have what you want” — Plautus
119: Imperātīvus et Iussīvus (Commands and Jussive) = Imperāre sibi maximum imperium est — “To command oneself is the greatest command” — Seneca
120: Hōrae (Telling Time) = Omnia tempus habent — “All things have their time” — Ecclesiastes
121: Tempus cum Partibus (Time with Fractions) = Tempus fugit — “Time flies” — Virgil
122: Mēnsēs (Months) = Omnium rerum principia parva sunt — “The beginnings of all things are small” — Cicero
123: Kalendae, Nōnae, Īdūs (Roman Dates) = Tempus edax rērum — “Time, devourer of all things” — Ovid
124: Nōmina et Ordō (Names and Order) = Nōmen est nōmen — “A name is a name” — Plautus
125: Dē Multiplicī Temporis Dictiōne (Complex Time Expressions) = Varium et mūtābile semper tempus — “Time is always changing” — Virgil
126: Quamdiū / Quamdūdum / Ā Quō Tempore (Duration) = Tempus omnia revelat — “Time reveals all things” — Seneca
127: Quōmodo Causa Exprimātur — Igitur = Ergo factum est — “Therefore it happened” — Cicero
128: Quōmodo Causa Exprimātur — Enim = Rēs ipsa loquitur — “The thing itself speaks” — Cicero
129: Quōmodo Causa Exprimātur — Cum / Quoniam = Quoniam ita est — “Since it is so” — Cicero
130: Affectūs (Emotions) = Omnia vincit amor — “Love conquers all” — Virgil
140: Dē Prōpōsitiōnibus Complētīvīs (Object Clauses) = Scīre est meminisse — “To know is to remember” — Cicero
141: Dē Instrūmentō et Modō (Means and Manner) = Ars longa, vita brevis — “Art is long, life is short” — Hippocrates
142: Dē Gaudiō et Spē (Joy, Shame, Hope, Fear) = Spēs ultima dea — “Hope is the last goddess” — Seneca
143: Dē Prōpōsitō (Purpose) = Finis coronat opus — “The end crowns the work” — Ovid
144: Dē Coniectūrā (General Truths) = Usus est magister optimus — “Practice is the best teacher” — Cicero
